The money's all in the back end. It's in the service department. And so it's all in the trickle of the customer after the big ticket sale. And so what's the stored value? What is it that exists in your customers, one thing, or exists in your product, another, that isn't being capitalized on? One way to get at it with product was Cosman's whole business with different distribution. So Joe Cosman, and if you haven't studied Joe Cosman, he's worth a look. Old, well actually dead, but old material, it's as old as you can get. You don't get any older. It's the ultimate anti-aging product. It's a casket. But so Joe made fortunes, one right after the other. So his theory, which is still true today, a little less so, but still true today, his theory was that most manufacturers of products, inventors of products, publishers, Melvin Power's got rich this way too, stop with about three ways of selling that product. So their distribution is very limited, right? But the product could be sold in other avenues of distribution, right? So if you think about like what Home Shopping TV did, see it's a whole new avenue of distribution for all kinds of products, right? So most jewelry, how was most jewelry sold? If you were a jewelry manufacturer, you sold jewelry to jewelry stores, you sold jewelry maybe to catalog companies, maybe to department stores, we're done, right? But Home Shopping, whole different distribution channel. So Joe was brilliant at seeing a product that could move to a different distribution channel, either as is or with very little modification. The biggest hit was the ant farm. So you would know about this, but if they're my age, we all had one. How many in here at an ant farm? Turn around and look, it's education. Oh, come on, you didn't have an ant farm. Where the hell did you get an ant farm? My grandmother. Your grandmother, yeah, okay. You could leave him home, you know, in the future. I'll be depressed by the time I get out of here. So you know what the ant farm is. So those of you who don't know, the ant farm is a little plastic doohickey with dirt and tunnels in it, and you can look in, you can see the ants running around. Well, it was originally sold to schools. Okay, the manufacturers thought that was a thing you sold to classrooms for teachers to use in biology class. And that's the only place they were selling it. So Joe spotted it in a school supply catalog. And he said, kids would love to have this. The prospect of ants getting loose in the house is, you know, it's almost irresistible. I mean, well, that's why kids like hamsters and gerbils and rats and snakes. I mean, same reason, right? Frogs. It's not they like, they want them to get loose. So he took the ant farm and made a toy out of it, which now, of course, opens up toy stores predominantly, mail order catalogs. Today, of course, all sorts of other distribution. So the question is, have we got something we're only selling one way, two ways, three ways that could be sold another way? Harley Davidson's having garage parties to sell motorcycles to women. So they're doing Tupperware parties for motorcycles. Doctors did it for Botox. Right in the early days of Botox. Home parties. Probably at first, I suspect, maybe in medical, unless you go all the way back, you know, to the Charlotte in days. But so different forms of distribution for the same thing, right? Those are all examples of unexploited opportunity or unused capacity. Almost everybody's got one or two or maybe more. And so how do you use the asset? You already got to get more out of it, right? Another, a biggie, and it treads on territory we usually live in a lot. So I'm going to force myself to zip. But another big place where you can use, where you can often find a great multiplier is in an altered means of selling. So think about what I said this morning about what Turner brought to multi-level that was not there before. So the means of selling, recruiting, if you will, before was just an adaptation of party plan. It was the living room meeting run by an amateur. He changed the means of selling the opportunity to professionally produced, professionally run large hotel meetings by corporate staff and field leaders. Corporate regionals combined with field leaders doing the meetings and everybody bringing the guests they would normally have invited to their living room over into a big room. It's an altered means of selling, nothing else changed, turbo charged the whole business for a whole host of reasons.
